petitioner_background: The petitioner is a cardiologist currently engaged in research on Takotsubo cardiomyopathy (TC), seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability in the sciences under INA section 203(b)(1)(A). He completed fellowships at two medical centers and has authored seven published articles, with research focused on differentiating Takotsubo cardiomyopathy from acute myocardial infarction (ACI) through electrocardiographic (ECG) changes. He served as principal investigator on multiple research studies, one of which won a prize for a poster presentation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted emails confirming the completion of peer review of at least two scientific articles submitted for publication to medical journals.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director found that the Petitioner satisfied the plain language of this criterion. Upon de novo review, the AAO agreed that the petitioner provided evidence of his peer review of scholarly articles submitted for publication in medical journals, and therefore satisfied the plain language of this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted eight testimonial letters from other cardiology researchers discussing his work on TC and its differentiation from ACI, including letters from a chairman, a chairman emeritus of a Department of Medicine, a director/medical director, and a staff cardiac electrophysiologist. He also submitted citation index evidence showing 20 total citations across his publications (18 claimed as independent citations), with seven articles total and three having been cited — the most heavily cited article receiving 10 citations and three articles collectively receiving 16 citations. He also cited his role as principal investigator on two research studies (one of which won a prize for a poster presentation), and the publication of his articles in prestigious journals. His research involved reviewing cases of cardiology patients during the five years prior to the study and measuring ECG changes in TC patients, finding them distinctly different from those in a typical heart attack.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner failed to meet this criterion. While the testimonial letters explained the originality of the work, none described in definite terms any measurable impact on the field of cardiology. The letters from the chairman, chairman emeritus, director, and cardiac electrophysiologist all praised the research as important and helpful to cardiologists but failed to identify any specific physicians, cardiologists, or institutions that had appropriated or implemented the petitioner's findings to aid in diagnosing TC. The AAO noted that letters from experts are not presumptive evidence of eligibility and USCIS may give less weight to opinions not corroborated by other information. Regarding the citation record, the AAO found the petitioner had not demonstrated that the level of citation was commensurate with an original contribution of major significance, had not shown a notable number of citing authors placed unusual reliance on his work, and had not identified citing authors who singled out his publications as significantly notable. On his role as principal investigator and the poster presentation prize, the AAO held that leading research projects and poster presentations do not in themselves constitute original contributions of major significance apart from the nature and impact of the research. On publication in prestigious journals, the AAO held that while notable, journal prestige reflects the overall quality of research but does not demonstrate influence of any particular author or impact within the field. The AAO rejected the argument that the prevalence of cardiovascular disease made his work significant, holding the burden was on the petitioner to demonstrate both originality and impact of major significance.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner authored seven articles that were published in professional journals.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director found, and the AAO agreed, that the petitioner provided evidence of his authorship of scholarly articles published in professional journals, satisfying the plain language of this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of two fellowships he held at prestigious medical centers and the research he conducted during that time, claiming critical roles for the Division of Cardiovascular Diseases at one institution and the Department of Cardiology at another. He relied on testimony contained in five support letters, including from an assistant professor of medicine in the Division of Cardiovascular Medicine who documented his progress and success during his advanced heart failure training fellowship beginning in 2013, noting he was first author of the research paper 'Electrocardiographic changes in Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy.' Another letter (Dr. Lichstein) noted the petitioner 'possesses an outstanding fund of medical knowledge,' was a skilled bedside clinician, and was first author of 2 publications co-authored by the letter writer. His direct supervisor stated he would 'explain [the Petitioner's] critical role' and that the petitioner worked as a resident from July 2009 to June 2012 and had been a Cardiology fellow since July 2014, was 'an integral part' of the department, had clinical skills and research that 'greatly helped to enhance our work,' and led multiple research projects as principal investigator.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner failed to meet this criterion. The AAO held that a leading role should be apparent by its position in the overall organizational hierarchy with matching duties, and a critical role should be apparent from the petitioner's impact on the organization's activities. None of the five letter writers claimed that the petitioner performed in a lead or critical role for either medical center or their cardiology departments. The assistant professor detailed aspects of advanced heart failure training but did not claim a lead or critical role. Dr. Lichstein praised the petitioner's knowledge and clinical skills but did not attribute a lead or critical role or claim the petitioner made a specific impact on the overall organization. Another letter spoke of the fellowship as 'an extra year of experience and training' without claiming a lead or critical role. While the direct supervisor stated the petitioner's clinical skills and research 'greatly enhanced' the department's work, he did not explain how the role was critical to the hospital or department, nor explain the impact of the research upon the hospital or whether it was critical to either entity's mission. The letters described activities involving advanced education in cardiology but did not describe duties commensurate with a lead role or specify how the petitioner contributed in ways significant to the organizations' outcome. The AAO rejected the petitioner's argument that it was 'an impractical standard to hold a practicing doctor to a hospital's top executives or surgeons,' noting the Director had indicated the record lacked organizational charts documenting the petitioner's position in the general hierarchy and lacked evidence that the petitioner was responsible for the hospital's success or standing.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner satisfied only two of the required three regulatory criteria (judging and scholarly articles), failing to meet both the original contributions criterion and the leading or critical role criterion that he challenged on appeal. Although the AAO noted it need not provide a final merits determination since the petitioner did not satisfy three criteria, it stated that a review of the record in the aggregate supported a finding that the petitioner had not established the level of expertise required for the classification sought, had not shown sustained national or international acclaim, and had not demonstrated he is one of the small percentage who has risen to the very top of the field of endeavor. The appeal was dismissed with each reason considered as an independent and alternate basis for the decision.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28 (3d Cir. 1995)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg'l Comm'r 1972)
